section_text: They show clear awareness of the game horizon but do not drastically change their contribution behavior at the end. Contributions in the final phase remain within the same low-to-mid band as earlier, without any sharp collapse or spike in cooperation. This indicates that they treat the end game as a continuation of their established strategy rather than an opportunity to either fully defect or demonstrate late altruism. Where they do show an end-game shift is in their use of punishment: as the game approaches its conclusion, they are willing to invest more heavily in sanctioning. Since there is no future interaction to recoup these costs, this late escalation implies that punishment is not purely instrumental for future-payoff incentives; they likely have a preference for norm enforcement or for expressing disapproval, even when there is little scope for future deterrence. In sum, their end-game pattern is: maintain established contribution levels, but loosen payoff-maximizing constraints on punishment, suggesting stable cooperative caution but stronger willingness to “settle accounts” as the interaction ends.

The player shows limited but noticeable horizon awareness. For most of the game, they behave as if cooperation is to be maintained regardless of time remaining. Near the end, they continue to fully cooperate even when others reduce their contributions, and they still spend on rewarding high contributors shortly before the final round. Only in the very last decision do they slightly reduce their own contribution instead of cleanly switching to defection. This suggests they recognize the approaching end but do not abandon their cooperative identity or shift into an aggressively self-interested mode. There is no systematic decay of contributions; instead, they preserve the cooperative norm and only marginally relax it at the final opportunity, implying that maintaining a cooperative pattern matters to them even when future reciprocation is no longer possible.

They show awareness of the finite horizon when it is mentioned by others but do not alter their strategy to exploit the last rounds. Contributions stay maximally cooperative through the final period, including the very last decision, despite the clear opportunity to defect with minimal future repercussions. They do not participate in the emerging “last round reflection” and “goodbye” chatter beyond having already emphasized earlier that cooperation is rational; at the end they still neither punish nor reward. There is no sign of cooperation decay or end‑game opportunism: their behavior remains consistent with the same payoff‑maximization logic they voiced earlier, treating the final round similarly to earlier ones. Any subtle shift in beliefs or emotions about others at the end is unknown; their actions suggest stable norms rather than horizon‑driven recalculation.

They show clear awareness of the finite horizon once it is pointed out but do not exploit it. Contributions remain maximal through the last rounds, including the final one, with no sign of deliberate last-round defection. Their chat in the final stage shifts toward closure and social niceties (“pleasure playing,” “gg”) rather than strategic recalculation, indicating they prioritize maintaining a positive group narrative over squeezing out minor extra personal gains. They do not suddenly increase punishment or reward intensity at the end; their behavior in sanctioning stays modest and consistent with their earlier pattern. Overall, horizon awareness does not erode their cooperation; they stick to the cooperative identity they have established.

They show full awareness of the horizon: they comment on last-round possibilities (e.g., collective heavy punishment, wondering about extreme outcomes for the low contributor) and respond to others’ “last round” remarks. Despite this awareness, they do not reduce their contribution in the final stages; they maintain full cooperation through the last round. They consider escalating punishment as a theoretical last-round move but do not follow through, suggesting that the idea is more exploratory/joking than a binding plan. There is no observable end-game unraveling: cooperation stays maximal, and they keep their behavior aligned with early-round norms. Their end-game orientation combines playful speculation with a consistent cooperative strategy, indicating that their identity as a cooperator outweighs short-term exploitative temptations even when future repercussions disappear.

They show no classic end-game defection: contribution remains at the maximum through the final rounds, including the last one, despite explicit acknowledgment by others that some players are persistently greedy and that “it makes little sense to donate.” There is no observable adjustment of behavior in anticipation of the game’s termination; horizon awareness is unknown from their own words, but action-wise they behave as if either time horizon is not salient or as if they normatively prefer to stick with the cooperative rule regardless of the approaching end. They do not shift into short-term payoff maximization, nor do they attempt last-minute exploitation of cooperative others. Their stability suggests a “rule-based cooperator” type rather than a forward-looking payoff optimizer in late stages.

They show no end-game deterioration in cooperation. Their contribution level remains maximally cooperative through the final round, despite the finite horizon and the absence of future interaction. This indicates either a strong internal norm for contributing or a forward-looking efficiency mindset that they do not relax even when instrumental future benefits disappear. Their final chat message is playful rather than strategic, and there is no sign of shifting to exploitation or of punishing prior free-riders in the last opportunity. Horizon awareness is unknown; they can see the remaining rounds, but their stable pattern suggests that awareness does not alter their behavior. They essentially treat the last round like any other in terms of contribution.

There is no visible end-game shift in strategy. The player starts with full defection and simply maintains it through all rounds, including the final ones. They show no sign of “last-round” experimentation or exploitation beyond what they already do from the beginning. Their behavior suggests they anchor on the stage-game dominant strategy from the outset and do not adjust it as the remaining-rounds count declines. Horizon awareness is present in the interface but leaves no detectable imprint on their choices; they neither ramp up nor decrease their already maximal free-riding.

Awareness of the finite horizon has little visible effect on their contribution strategy: they continue to give fully up to and including the very last round. There is no systematic end-game fade-out of cooperation. The single defection occurs somewhat late, but since they immediately return to full contributions and sustain them to the end, it does not resemble a planned end-game unraveling. They also do not “cash out” by reducing punishment vulnerability or by escalating punitive activity near the end; their overall pattern remains similar. This suggests that they either discount backward-induction logic, prioritize norm-consistent behavior over end-of-game payoffs, or do not strongly integrate horizon information into their choices. Any explicit belief about the game ending or about others defecting in the final rounds is unknown.

The player shows clear horizon awareness and exhibits classic end-game defection. When others stop contributing and the game is close to ending, they fully revert to non-cooperation, aligning their choices with the strictly dominant strategy as the remaining opportunity for repeated interaction shrinks. They do not attempt any last-round cooperative gesture or signaling; instead, they treat the end of the game as a reason to eliminate contribution risk altogether. There is no sign of escalation of cooperation in late stages; rather, cooperation decays as the end approaches and as mutual cooperation becomes less likely. This pattern suggests they understand that future rounds are limited and adjust toward self-interest when long-term reciprocal benefits are no longer plausible.

There is no clear evidence of a strong classic end-game collapse. They continue to contribute non-trivial amounts through the final rounds rather than dropping to zero. Their pattern late in the game remains mixed: sometimes low, sometimes moderate, with no simple monotonic decay. This suggests they are aware of the horizon but do not fully switch into purely selfish extraction at the end. They seem to maintain a “responsive” mindset through the final rounds instead of a strict backward-induction logic. They keep adjusting to what others appear to be doing rather than explicitly exploiting the very last opportunity. There is no observable escalation of punishment or reward in the final round, nor any special payoff-grab move that stands out. Their strategy at the end looks similar in spirit to earlier behavior: somewhat cooperative, somewhat opportunistic, and responsive to recent history rather than to the abstract fact that the game is about to end. Overall, this indicates limited horizon-driven planning and a stronger reliance on myopic or short-run conditional strategies.

This player shows horizon awareness in the sense that they experience very low payoffs when others reduce contributions late in the game, yet they still continue to contribute at or near their usual high levels. Toward the end, they maintain or return to high contributions rather than shifting into end-game defection, and there is no clear decay of cooperation as the final rounds approach. Even when group contributions become more volatile and the personal return from giving is temporarily poor, they do not adopt a low-contribution end-game strategy. This pattern indicates either a preference-based commitment to cooperation that outweighs short-term payoff optimization, or a belief that sustaining high contributions is still worthwhile even with few rounds remaining. They appear relatively insensitive to the classic end-game logic and keep acting in line with their earlier cooperative norm.

There is no clear evidence of classic end-game defection: they maintain full contributions through to the final round, including when others defect near the end. They do not appear to alter their contribution behavior in anticipation of the game ending, despite clearly knowing the number of rounds. There is some late-game focus on cleaning up coordination and mild joking about punishing a chronic free-rider, but this does not translate into abandoning cooperation. Across late rounds, they continue to endorse cooperative norms verbally and behaviorally. Any subtle adjustments in their use of punishment near the end are not strongly interpretable from the transcript alone. Overall, their end-game pattern is consistent with a player whose strategy is guided more by a stable cooperative norm and desire for smooth group play than by backward induction or short-term exploitation.

The player shows no decay in cooperation as the game approaches the final round. They continue with full contributions through the end, suggesting they are either not using backward-induction logic or consciously reject it in favor of consistency with cooperative norms. Instead of conserving resources in the last round, they even increase costly prosocial actions at the end, implying that end-game awareness does not lead them to disengage but may actually strengthen their desire to act in line with their cooperative identity.

Awareness of the time horizon does not change their contribution behavior; they free-ride from the outset and continue unchanged through the final round. There is no end-game decay in cooperation because there is no cooperation at any point. If there is any end-game effect, it appears only (and weakly) in their reward decisions, not in their willingness to contribute. Overall, they treat the game as a finite sequence in which contributing is never privately optimal and never worth trying, even when others remain cooperative late in the game. Their horizon awareness is therefore either minimal or strategically irrelevant to their core choice about contributing.

There is no visible end-game unraveling in their behavior. They continue to contribute fully through the last stretch of the game, including the final rounds, despite knowing how many rounds remain. Their strategy does not become more selfish or more punitive as the horizon approaches. Instead, they treat late rounds as functionally identical to earlier cooperative phases when the group is in full-contribution equilibrium. This implies that they either do not rely on backward induction or deliberately ignore its selfish logic in favor of maintaining consistency with a cooperative rule. They do not attempt last-minute exploitation of the group, nor do they escalate punishment near the end to settle scores or express moral outrage. Their approach to the end game is “steady-state cooperation,” not opportunistic adjustment.

The player shows awareness of the game horizon but does not exhibit a simple, monotonic end‑game decay. Their contributions fluctuate in later rounds rather than collapsing to pure free‑riding. They sometimes reduce contributions when the game is nearing its close, but they also return to very high giving in the final round. This pattern suggests they do not fully adopt backward‑induction logic; instead they continue to use a responsive, context‑based rule even when only a few rounds remain. They may view the final period as an opportunity for an expressive or “last cooperative” move, or they may simply follow a habit of matching what appears to be rising or stable cooperation among others, rather than strategically exploiting the terminal round. Overall, horizon effects are present but weak and inconsistent. Confidence in their underlying reasoning about the horizon is low; only the behavioral fact of non‑collapse and a late cooperative spike is clear.

The player clearly shows horizon awareness. For most of the game, they maintain cooperative contributions despite evidence of imperfect group cooperation and despite observing a profitable, persistent free-rider. Only in the final period do they stop contributing, at a point when the remaining-round counter reaches zero afterward and there is no opportunity for future reciprocity or reputation building. This late defection is consistent with an end-game effect: once they can no longer use current behavior to influence future play or rewards, they relax their cooperative stance and take advantage of the public good. They do not appear to escalate cooperation to “end on a high note”; instead, they shift toward self-payoff maximization at the last opportunity. This pattern suggests a player who values cooperation as a repeated-game strategy but expects it to unravel at the terminal round, and is willing to participate in that unraveling. Their end-game choice also implies that any internal norm of “always contribute” is conditional on the presence of ongoing interaction.

Awareness of the finite horizon does not change their cooperative behavior. They maintain full contributions through the final round and do not exhibit end-game decay, even when others reduce or withdraw cooperation near the end. This indicates that backward induction or last-round payoff maximization is not guiding their decisions. They do not appear to “punish” end-game defections by cutting their own contributions. If they adjust beliefs about others near the end, this does not manifest in changed contributions. Any internal reasoning about the end game is unknown, but behaviorally they act as if cooperation is a persistent norm independent of horizon.

They exhibit strong horizon awareness but no cooperation decay. They clearly understand that the game’s structure allows change and that behavior in later rounds could differ; they explicitly note that any change would come from players’ own decisions and that the situation may be set up to see whether participants will defect. Despite this awareness, they do not reduce contributions near the end, nor do they even experiment with deviation. Instead, their framing of possible end-game defection uses moral language (“sneaks”), which suggests they morally disapprove of last-minute free-riding and reject it as a behavioral option for themselves. As the game approaches the final rounds, their communication becomes more reflective and then turns into a group-oriented farewell, maintaining cooperative consistency through the very last decision. There is no sign of last-round exploitation, testing, or hedging; they treat the cooperative pattern as something to sustain until the formal end, regardless of strategic temptation.

They display no detectable end‑game defection or decay in cooperation. Full contributions continue unchanged through the final rounds, including the very last one. They appear aware that the game has a fixed horizon (they react to mentions of remaining rounds and bonus) but do not translate this into strategic withholding. They also do not join the late‑game “revenge” or mass‑punishment campaign against the chronic defector, even when others explicitly call for it. This suggests an end‑game profile of “principled cooperator”: strategy remains the same regardless of remaining rounds, and they are not tempted by last‑round grabs or expressive punishment.

The player does not display a simple end-game defection pattern. They do not monotonically decrease contributions toward the final round; instead they end on a cooperative choice. This indicates that they are at least aware that the horizon is short (structurally visible), yet they are not treating the final stage purely as an opportunity for last-round exploitation. Their final behavior suggests either a stable, if flexible, preference for some level of cooperation, or a desire to re-align with what they perceive as a cooperative norm rather than to cash out via defection. Whether they are consciously thinking about future reputation or repeated interactions is unknown, since anonymity and lack of future interaction signals do not prevent them from ending cooperatively.

The player does not exhibit end-game defection; instead, they escalate to maximum cooperation in the final round once a strong cooperative consensus forms. They show awareness that the game has a short, known horizon but do not use this as an excuse to free-ride at the end. Instead, they appear willing to treat the last round as an opportunity to realize the full benefits of coordination. Their behavior suggests they are not purely backward-inducting; they are willing to cooperate fully in the final period if they believe others will do the same. Any sophisticated strategic adjustment seems driven more by expectations about group behavior and established norms than by the looming end of the game.

They continue to contribute fully through the entire horizon, including in the final rounds when both partners defect and the cooperative return is clearly worse. There is no observable end-game decay in cooperation: their contribution rule remains unchanged even when only a few rounds remain and mutual defection by others is stable. This indicates either a lack of strategic horizon reasoning (they do not adjust to the finite end of the game) or a deliberate commitment to a principle of cooperation that overrides typical end-game backward induction. They show no last-round opportunism and do not try to recoup losses or exploit others when the game is about to end. Their reward behavior does not show a distinct end-game shift either, but because they rarely reward overall and reward decisions already appear cautious, it is difficult to infer a specific end-game pattern there (low confidence).

The player shows no end-game decay in cooperation. Their contributions remain maximal right through the final decision, even after the group explicitly notes that the game is ending and comments on the small eventual payoffs. Awareness of the horizon is present in the environment and referenced in chat, but this does not translate into any detectable strategic shift by the player. They do not exploit the last rounds to defect, do not attempt a final-round payoff grab, and do not mirror other players’ small increases in opportunism towards the end. Their contribution pattern in the final rounds is indistinguishable from their pattern earlier in the game. This indicates either an absence of backward-induction reasoning in their applied strategy or, more plausibly, a priority for consistency with their cooperation norm over any end-game payoff advantage. They appear not to use the knowledge that “this is the last chance” to modify behavior; instead, they preserve the same rule they follow all along: always contribute fully. There is no terminal communication from them, no goodbye, and no reflective comment on the game. In new PGG environments, this suggests that even with clear finite horizons and visible end-game opportunities to defect, they continue to behave as a horizon-agnostic, full cooperator unless some external mechanism explicitly forces or heavily rewards deviation.

Awareness of the finite horizon does not lead them to defect at the end. They maintain high contributions in later rounds and do not relax their punishment stance as the game closes. There is no observable end-game decay in cooperation; if anything, they continue to act in line with their established cooperative and punitive pattern. They appear motivated by norm enforcement and a preference for cooperation that is stable across the game rather than by backward induction or short-term payoff maximization in the final stages. Any subtle variations late in the game are small; they do not shift into opportunistic low contributions even when the opportunity is greatest.

They show no observable end-game defection. Their maximum contributions and willingness to pay for punishment persist through the final round, without reduction. Awareness of the horizon does not appear to weaken their cooperative or punitive stance; if anything, their target set for punishment stays focused on low contributors in the last round as well. There is no sign of strategic “cashing out” or relaxing norms toward the end.

They show no end-game decay in cooperation. Their maximal contribution rule persists through to the final round despite the finite horizon and the presence of repeated free-riding and punishment. They do not exploit the last round for personal gain, indicating either a principled commitment to cooperation, an indifference to end-game strategic logic, or both. There is no observable shift toward self-interest as the game approaches termination, and no indication that awareness of the remaining number of rounds influences their strategy.

There is no visible end-game defection: the player maintains identical, maximal contributions up to and including the final round. They show horizon awareness in the sense that the interface reveals the number of remaining rounds, but their strategy does not change as the game approaches termination. They do not attempt to exploit the final rounds by free-riding, nor do they escalate to any new pattern of punishment or reward at the end. This suggests that either they disregard backward-induction logic, they prioritize consistency of cooperative identity over end-game payoff optimization, or they expect that maintaining cooperation to the end is normatively “correct.” Their end-game response is thus steady-state cooperation with no decay and no terminal “grab.”

The player is clearly horizon-aware: they know how many rounds remain, and their behavior near the end includes free-riding in the final interaction. This indicates at least some sensitivity to end-game incentives and a willingness to deviate from cooperation when there is no future interaction to sustain reputational or reciprocal benefits. However, their pattern is not a simple, monotonic end-game unraveling. They return to full contribution just before the end, and then free-ride again when the game concludes. This mix suggests that they understand the advantage of defecting when future consequences are absent, but they do not implement a pure backward-induction strategy. Instead, they treat the final round as an opportunistic chance to maximize private payoff given that others have maintained high contributions. There is no observable emotional or communicative reaction to the game ending: they neither express regret, justification, nor triumph. Their response to the end game is purely behavioral and instrumental: they maintain general cooperation while it remains collectively beneficial and socially supported, but they are willing to defect quietly at the terminal stage to capture additional payoff, without attempting to influence others’ behavior through chat.

They show clear awareness of cumulative earnings and track their own total, but they do not alter their strategy as the game nears its end. There is no end-game defection: they maintain full cooperation in all observed rounds, including the final ones, despite knowing how many rounds remain. They remain socially engaged near the end, commenting on totals earlier and offering a friendly goodbye at the conclusion, which suggests they continue to view the interaction as a cooperative group effort rather than a finite-horizon opportunity to exploit others. Their behavior is consistent with a type that either values norm consistency and group harmony over last-round opportunism, or has internalized full contribution as the “obvious” best response given the established group norm.

Awareness of the finite horizon does not lead this player to unravel their cooperation. Near the end, they continue to contribute fully in most rounds, with only a single late-stage free-ride that is not followed by sustained defection. There is no clear pattern of cooperation decay: they preserve their baseline cooperative stance through to the final round. This suggests that they either discount end-game logic, prioritize stable norms, or believe others will also persist in cooperation regardless of the approaching finish. They do not appear to use the final rounds to exploit others systematically or to launch revenge or reward campaigns; instead, they maintain consistency in both contributions and lack of sanctioning, indicating a preference for a stable strategy over horizon-sensitive optimization.

The player is clearly aware of the finite horizon and behaves in line with backward‑induction logic. As the game progresses and the remaining number of rounds shrinks, their behavior converges almost entirely to free‑riding. In the late and final part of the game, they largely stop experimenting with contributions and settle into keeping their full endowment even when others still contribute considerable amounts. This indicates that they discount the value of reputation or future reciprocity when few or no rounds remain. Knowing that future interactions are limited or about to end removes any residual incentive they might have to test or sustain cooperative norms. There is no sign of an end‑game “cooperation spike” or last‑round gift; instead, they maintain individually optimal defection through to the conclusion. This suggests a pragmatic, payoff‑focused mindset: once the prospect of long‑term gains from cooperation becomes negligible, they see no reason to sacrifice current earnings, even symbolically.

They show consistent awareness of the finite horizon; they can see how many rounds remain, and they do not adjust their strategy as the end approaches. Their contributions remain fully cooperative through the final rounds, with no observable end-game decay in cooperation. In the late game, they keep participating in the social narrative about bots and about “victory,” but their own decisions do not become more selfish or cautious, even when the opportunity to defect at low social cost is maximal. This suggests that their cooperation is not driven by expectation of future reciprocation in this particular game, but by a stable internal rule or preference for contributing in such environments. Near the end, they continue to make light, reflective comments about how bots might have behaved more cleverly, indicating a reflective, somewhat playful stance toward the whole experience rather than a narrow payoff-focus. They do not exhibit last-round opportunism, spite, or resignation; instead, they finish in line with their initial norm: contribute fully and treat the game as a cooperative, slightly amusing social exercise. In summary, across the end-game phase they maintain principled full cooperation, preserve a relaxed, humorous tone in chat, and show no shift toward strategic defection despite knowing the interaction is about to end.

They show no end-game unraveling: their contribution strategy remains unchanged through the final rounds. They continue to fully cooperate even after some mid/late-game deviations by others and despite the clear visibility of the approaching final round. They do not express concerns about others potentially defecting at the end, nor do they attempt to preemptively protect themselves or to warn the group about end-game incentives. This suggests either (a) they rely on a simple rule that disregards horizon effects when cooperation is highly profitable, or (b) they assume that the group’s established norm and mutual understanding are strong enough to persist through the end. There is no evidence of strategic escalation or last-round experimentation; they appear content to maintain the status quo cooperative pattern until the game concludes. Their affect in late chat remains light and social rather than strategic, implying low anxiety about end-game exploitation.

They are horizon-aware and adjust behavior in the last third of the game. Early and mid-game, their pattern is stable, with near-uniform full contributions and steady, moderate use of punishment. In the late game, they experiment with defection while most others still cooperate, and then return to full contribution afterward. The timing and limited frequency of those defections indicate they are not fully abandoning cooperation, but they do relax their norm adherence as the remaining rounds shrink. They do not escalate punishment in the final rounds; instead, their punishment intensity remains within their usual moderate range. This combination points to a “soft end-game effect”: they opportunistically test defection near the end but do not switch into a pure money-maximizing or spiteful mode, and they still behave as a norm enforcer right up to the last round. <PU N ISHMENT> They are a consistent, moderately costly norm enforcer who targets low contributors rather than spending heavily or randomly. Across the game they regularly spend nontrivial amounts on punishment, but they typically keep their own payoffs positive and never pursue the extreme, self-destructive punishment levels that some others display. This indicates they care both about norm enforcement and about their own earnings. Their punishment is selective and strongly aligned with contribution behavior. They reliably punish players who contribute nothing when that behavior reduces the group’s total contribution, and they continue to do so even when some free-riders are already being punished by others, suggesting they have an internal fairness standard rather than purely free-riding on others’ enforcement. When everyone contributes fully, their punishment expenditure does not drop to zero but remains at a low-to-moderate level, likely reflecting attempts to reinforce norms against chronic defectors who have a history of low contributions in earlier rounds. There is no clear evidence of direct retaliation for being punished themselves: they sometimes punish in rounds where they are also punished, but targets are consistent with contribution-based criteria rather than matching who just imposed costs on them (which they cannot directly observe). Their pattern looks more like moralistic punishment aimed at maintaining cooperation than vendetta-driven behavior. They do not appear to use punishment strategically to gain relative advantage over other high contributors; they do not systematically attack cooperators or high earners. Instead, their punishment falls more on chronic or recurrent low contributors. This supports interpreting them as a “principled enforcer” rather than a competitively spiteful player.

Awareness of the game horizon does not change their contribution behavior; they remain a strict zero-contributor through early, middle, and final rounds. There is no cooperation decay because there is never any cooperation to begin with. Punishment behavior also does not soften near the end: they continue to spend on punishment, including in the final rounds, rather than, for example, hoarding all remaining payoffs. This indicates that end-game considerations (such as “nothing matters now” or “no future deterrence is possible”) do not strongly weaken their willingness to impose costly sanctions. If anything, their pattern suggests that their strategy is rule-like and not dynamically tuned to the remaining number of rounds.

There is no visible end‑game unraveling in their contribution behavior. They maintain full cooperation through the final phase and into the very last round, without any sign of tapering off or switching to free‑riding when the horizon is short. Their two free‑riding episodes occur in the middle of the game rather than clustered near the end, which provides no evidence that they deliberately exploit the final rounds. Horizon awareness is present in the game design but does not manifest as a strategic change in this player’s pattern. They behave as if cooperation is a round‑by‑round norm to uphold rather than something to unwind as the game closes.

The player demonstrates awareness of the game horizon in the sense that they see the remaining rounds, but their end‑phase pattern does not show a clear, monotonic unraveling of cooperation. Near the end, they still participate in full‑contribution episodes and then intermittently drop to lower contributions, including in the final round. This last‑round deviation from the fully cooperative pattern aligns with a mild end‑game effect: when the future no longer matters, they are more willing to free‑ride. However, this behavior is not unique to the final round; they adopt similar opportunistic drops earlier as well. Thus, the end‑game effect is present but not dominant. Their strategy remains mixed—periodic cooperation and exploitation—rather than converging to pure defection as the horizon approaches. They do not communicate anything about the game ending, so their internal beliefs about the horizon remain unknown.

Awareness of the finite horizon does not lead them to aggressively defect at the end. Instead, they maintain or return to high contributions in the closing phase. Even in the last few rounds, including the final one, they are willing to contribute fully when the environment looks collectively cooperative and punishment appears subdued or targeted at clear outliers. There is no visible last-round “grab” for private payoff. Their end-game pattern indicates either a preference-based commitment to cooperation (valuing fairness or group welfare intrinsically) or a heuristic of “cooperate as long as most others do,” independent of horizon length. If anything, their late-game behavior slightly polarizes: they sometimes under-contribute when the atmosphere becomes chaotic or unfair, but when the group re-coalesces around high contributions, they quickly align with that norm despite the short remaining horizon. They seem to prioritize ending on cooperative, high-payoff equilibria rather than exploiting final-round anonymity.

The player shows clear awareness of the finite horizon but does not adopt a classic end-game defection pattern. Near the end, they briefly reduce their contribution and then return to a high level in the final round when others also contribute strongly. This combination suggests they understand that the game is ending yet still value maintaining cooperation to the last round when group behavior justifies it. There is no visible collapse into zero or minimal giving as the final rounds approach. Instead, their behavior reflects a mild end-game adjustment: a temporary dip that could represent checking whether they can improve their own payoff now that the relationship is about to end, followed by renewed full cooperation when they see continued or increased contributions from others. This points to a player who balances strategic self-interest with a sustained cooperative identity, even under full horizon awareness.

This player is aware of the finite horizon and does not follow the classic backward-induction pattern of simply defecting at the end. Near the final period, they still attempt full contribution even when others have recently chosen not to cooperate, suggesting that they either (a) maintain a cooperative norm independent of horizon when they see any chance to improve group payoffs, or (b) are experimenting or following a rule-of-thumb that does not sharply discount the final round. The last-round choice shows no clear “end-game collapse” on their part; instead, their cooperation can persist even when others appear to exploit it. Their strategy therefore does not appear to be driven primarily by end-game pessimism, but by local assessments of others’ behavior, with some willingness to sustain cooperation despite approaching the final round. Motivation for this is unknown; it could reflect a stable preference for cooperation, a desire to maintain a self-image as a cooperator, or uncertainty about others’ strategies.

There is no clear evidence of a classic end‑game collapse driven purely by horizon awareness. Contributions vary throughout the game in a similar toggle pattern; zero contributions appear both in the early and later stages, and high contributions also persist into late rounds. Because both cooperative and non‑cooperative choices occur in early, middle, and late phases, any systematic end‑game decay in cooperation is at best weak and low‑confidence. Near the end, they alternate between zero and full contributions much as they do earlier. This suggests they do not rely on a simple “defect in the last rounds” heuristic. Instead, they continue to apply the same conditional, payoff‑oriented logic: they free‑ride when they perceive low cooperation or when they prioritize short‑term gain, and they continue to fully cooperate in some late rounds despite the short remaining horizon. Their horizon awareness is therefore unknown from behavior alone. The data are consistent with them understanding the number of remaining rounds but not fundamentally changing strategy because of it. Their pattern is better described as context‑ and payoff‑driven than as explicitly end‑game‑driven.

They are clearly aware of the finite horizon, explicitly noting the remaining number of rounds and responding when another player points out that the game is almost over. Their reaction to the approaching end is not to reduce contributions or cease rewarding but to escalate compensatory and “catch-up” rewarding, promising larger transfers to make up for earlier missed rewards. They express concern about time constraints and interface issues, and try to correct perceived past under-reciprocation even in the final rounds. There is no sign of last-round defection: they maintain maximum contributions and continue to push for high mutual rewards until the very end. They also take time to offer social closure (“good game,” saying they enjoyed it), which suggests they treat the end-game as a moment to affirm group identity and fairness rather than to exploit the absence of future interactions.

Awareness of the game horizon does not meaningfully weaken this player’s cooperative stance. Their contribution level remains high through the final phase, with no systematic decay as the last rounds approach. There is no visible transition into end-game defection even though such a shift would be individually profitable and is common in standard public goods settings. Instead, they maintain a consistent cooperative strategy, suggesting either that they discount the end-game logic or they deliberately resist it in favor of norm-consistent behavior.
